narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive strengths like your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, which is above average, and an LSAT score of 42 that stands out well beyond the typical 36.70. Your Year 1 school decile of 8 is also stronger than the average of about 5.7, reflecting solid early performance, while your upper-middle family income provides a supportive backdrop shared by fewer students. You're planning to attend full-time, just like over 90% of others in the dataset. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' bar exam outcomes, predicted a 62.6% chance you'll fail the bar on your first try—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes those likely to pass and succeed in practice. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your undergraduate GPA; even though it's solid, it had the strongest pull in the model's unfavorable direction for you specifically. Next in influence, your Year 3 school decile of 9—higher than the average 5.5—worked strongly in your favor, nudging the prediction toward passing. Your family income bracket then contributed toward the failure side, followed by your Year 1 decile adding a smaller push in that direction, with your LSAT offering a minor positive boost and full-time status barely factoring in. Compared to typical students, your profile shines in LSAT and early grades but aligns with patterns where later performance and background tipped the scales against bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—it's the model's data-driven call based on historical trends. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT for an even higher score, strengthening your recent academic decile through additional coursework, or gathering letters highlighting your resilience and bar prep commitment to appeal or reapply stronger next cycle. You've got the foundation; refining these key areas can shift the odds in your favor. Keep pushing—you're meant for this.
